games.explanation: Skenes is the best pitcher on the planet and the price still has to account for the fact that one start is one start, especially when the lineup behind him is the Pirates. Miami brings a left-handed-heavy order into a matchup where Skenes has been merely very good instead of untouchable, and Kyle Stowers is the type of barrel-everything bat (a 51.6% hard-hit rate) who can flip a low-scoring game with one swing. A 20 mph crosswind in a park that already suppresses homers points to exactly the tight, weird night that lets an underdog hang around. At +136 on a coin flip our numbers say leans Miami's way, you're getting paid to sit through nine innings of Skenes being Skenes.

Emerson Hancock is the kind of arm the market keeps pricing like he's solved something, when really he's just been handed a friendly schedule. He walks too many lefties to be laying chalk against a Washington lineup built around them, and James Wood in particular is the exact profile (lefty, hits the ball roughly as hard as anyone in the league) that turns Hancock's command issues into crooked numbers. Mikolas isn't winning any awards either, but a coin-flip game shouldn't be priced like a clear favorite, and the simulation has these teams dead even at 4.7 apiece. Plus money on the better matchup is the whole pitch.

The board is pricing this like the Dodgers are a lock and the White Sox are a formality, which ignores that Emmet Sheehan is still a rookie arm who gives left-handed bats a free pass any time he loses the zone. Chicago happens to run Benintendi and Vargas at the top, exactly the profile that turns Sheehan's near-13% walk rate against lefties into a multi-run inning. Fedde is no prize, but a one-run projected game against Ohtani and friends is closer to a coin flip than the price suggests, and the wind isn't helping anyone launch one out tonight. Plus-money on the better dog in a tight game is the whole pitch, and +164 pays you to be right once.
